constructionRule: Fixtures are exact stored Jobs rows whose InputValue is Captain-defined in Docs/AGENTS/benchmark-expectations.json.
No input paraphrasing, translation, synthetic evidence, or hand curation is applied.
ResultJson and latest AnalysisMetrics.MetricsJson are compacted to stage-facing structures, with source JSON hashes retained.
Fixture hashes are deterministic and exclude wall-clock export time.

claimBoundaries.name: Offizielle SEM-Bestandszählung aus administrativen Registern (ZEMIS), Ende 2024
claimBoundaries.description: Stichtagsbezogene Bestandszählungen des SEM aus administrativen Registern (ZEMIS) per 31. Dezember 2024. Umfasst verschiedene Teilbestände des Asylbereichs (Gesamtbestand inkl. RU, Bestand im Asylprozess, anerkannte Flüchtlinge, vorläufig Aufgenommene, vorübergehender Schutz). Gleiche Methodik, gleicher Zeitpunkt, gleiche Quelle — unterschiedliche analytische Dimensionen sind Teilmengen desselben Registers.

understanding.impliedClaim: Mehr als 235 000 Personen aus dem Asylbereich sind zurzeit in der Schweiz
understanding.backgroundDetails: Die Aussage bezieht sich auf die aktuelle Anzahl von Personen im Asylbereich, die sich in der Schweiz aufhalten. Dies umfasst verschiedene Kategorien wie Personen mit anerkanntem Schutzstatus (insbesondere Status S für ukrainische Geflüchtete), Schutzsuchende in laufenden Verfahren und andere Personen im Asylsystem.

gate1Reasoning.reasoning: This is a factual assertion about a quantifiable population (asylum-related persons in Switzerland). It is specific enough—it provides a concrete threshold (>235,000), a defined population category (asylum-related persons), a clear geographic scope (Switzerland), and a temporal anchor (currently/at present). The claim directly mirrors the original input without adding evidence-derived specifics or shifting to representational predicates. It passes all three gates.

contractValidationSummary.summary: The single extracted claim AC_01 is a verbatim restatement of the input, preserving all three priority anchors: the lower-bound quantifier 'Mehr als 235 000', the scope qualifier 'aus dem Asylbereich', and the temporal anchor 'zurzeit'. No normative injection, proxy drift, or structural issues detected.

truthConditionAnchor.anchorText: Mehr als 235 000 … aus dem Asylbereich … zurzeit
preservedByQuotes: Mehr als 235 000 Personen aus dem Asylbereich sind zurzeit in der Schweiz

anchors.rationale: The lower-bound quantifier 'more than' is constitutive to the claim; removing it would shift from a minimum threshold to an exact or different quantity, requiring different verification evidence.
anchors.truthConditionShiftIfRemoved: Without 'more than', the claim would require verification of a precise count rather than confirmation that the number exceeds 235,000.

anchors.rationale: The scope restriction to 'asylum-related' persons is essential; removing it would broaden the claim to all persons in Switzerland, requiring entirely different evidence.
anchors.truthConditionShiftIfRemoved: Without this scope qualifier, the claim would concern the total population in Switzerland rather than specifically asylum-related persons.

anchors.rationale: The temporal anchor 'currently' specifies the claim as present-state rather than historical or future; its removal would eliminate the time-specificity of the assertion.
anchors.truthConditionShiftIfRemoved: Without 'zurzeit', the claim would lack temporal grounding and could refer to any point in time, requiring different evidence about when the count applies.

claimSelection.rationale: Es liegt genau ein Kandidat vor, der die Artikelthese direkt widerspiegelt, eine hohe Spezifität aufweist und durch amtliche Quellen (SEM-Statistiken) mit hoher Wahrscheinlichkeit belegt oder widerlegt werden kann. Die Auswahl ist eindeu...

evidenceItems.statement: Das Staatssekretariat für Migration (SEM) verzeichnete im Januar 2026 1694 neue Asylgesuche, 15,9 % weniger als im Vormonat und 11,4 % weniger im Vergleich zum Vorjahresmonat.
evidenceItems.sourceExcerpt: Das Staatssekretariat für Migration (SEM) verzeichnete im Januar 2026 1694 neue Asylgesuche, 15,9 % weniger als im Vormonat und 11,4 % weniger im Vergleich zum Vorjahresmonat.

evidenceItems.statement: Im Januar 2026 hat das SEM über 2436 Asylgesuche entschieden, wovon 397 (19 %) gutgeheissen wurden.
evidenceItems.sourceExcerpt: Im Januar 2026 hat das SEM über 2436 Asylgesuche entschieden, wovon 397 (19 %) gutgeheissen wurden.

evidenceItems.statement: Im Januar 2026 haben 774 Personen ohne Aufenthaltsrecht die Schweiz kontrolliert verlassen, davon 502 selbstständig, während 272 Personen polizeilich in ihren Herkunftsstaat, den zuständigen Dublin-Staat oder einen Drittstaat begleitet wurden.
evidenceItems.sourceExcerpt: Im Januar 2026 haben 774 Personen ohne Aufenthaltsrecht die Schweiz kontrolliert verlassen, davon 502 selbstständig, während 272 Personen polizeilich in ihren Herkunftsstaat, den zuständigen Dublin-Staat oder einen Drittstaat begleitet wurden.

evidenceItems.statement: Das Staatssekretariat für Migration (SEM) publiziert monatliche Asylstatistiken mit Bestandeszahlen nach Unterprozess, Ausländergruppe, Asylsuchenden (Ausweis N), vorläufig Aufgenommenen (Ausweis F), anerkannten Flüchtlingen mit Asyl (Ausweis B) und Personen mit vorübergehendem Schutz (Ausweis S) für Mai 2026.
evidenceItems.sourceExcerpt: Das Staatssekretariat für Migration (SEM) publiziert monatliche Asylstatistiken mit Bestandeszahlen nach Unterprozess, Ausländergruppe, Asylsuchenden (Ausweis N), vorläufig Aufgenommenen (Ausweis F), anerkannten Flüchtlingen mit Asyl (Ausweis B) und Personen mit vorübergehendem Schutz (Ausweis S) für Mai 2026.

understanding.impliedClaim: The legal proceedings against Jair Bolsonaro complied with Brazilian law, and the proceedings and verdicts met international standards for a fair trial.
understanding.backgroundDetails: The input asks two coordinated questions about the Bolsonaro legal proceedings: (1) compliance with Brazilian domestic law, and (2) conformity with international fair-trial standards. These are distinct verifiable propositions—one anchored in Brazilian legal framework and procedure, the other in international human rights norms and comparative judicial standards. The preliminary evidence indicates the proceedings occurred in Brazil's Federal Supreme Court (STF) in 2024-2025, involved indictment on November 21, 2024, trial in September 2024, and conviction on September 11, 2025, on charges related to attempted coup. Evidence also surfaces procedural concerns (judicial impartiality, concentration of responsibilities, precautionary measures) and defenses challenging validity of evidence and proportionality of restrictions. The input does not specify which particular proceedings or verdicts (there may be multiple stages), so the claims address the proceedings and verdicts as a whole.
understanding.articleThesis: The legal proceedings against Jair Bolsonaro complied with Brazilian law, and the proceedings and verdicts met international standards for a fair trial.

atomicClaims.statement: The legal proceedings against Jair Bolsonaro complied with Brazilian law.

methodologies: Comparative analysis of STF procedures against Brazilian Criminal Procedure Code (CPP) and 1988 Constitution
Review of judicial opinions and procedural orders for compliance with statutory requirements
Expert legal assessment of jurisdictional authority, evidence collection, witness examination, and verdict issuance
expectedMetrics: Compliance with Article 102, I, 'b' of 1988 Brazilian Constitution (STF jurisdiction over federal representatives)
Adherence to Brazilian Criminal Code Articles 359-L and 359-M (applicable statutory charges)
Compliance with Law No. 14,197/2021 (democratic institutions protection framework)
Procedural regularity: indictment acceptance, evidence collection phases, witness testimony procedures, verdict issuance
Adherence to due process guarantees under Brazilian law (right to defense, access to evidence, appeal rights)
expectedEvidenceProfile.primaryMetric: Compliance of STF proceedings with Brazilian Criminal Procedure Code, 1988 Constitution, and applicable criminal statutes
componentMetrics: Jurisdictional authority under Article 102, I, 'b' of Constitution
Statutory applicability of Articles 359-L and 359-M of Criminal Code
Procedural regularity of indictment acceptance (March 26, 2025)
Evidence collection and witness examination procedures
Verdict issuance and legal reasoning (September 11, 2025)

atomicClaims.statement: The proceedings against Jair Bolsonaro met international standards for a fair trial.

methodologies: Comparative analysis of STF proceedings against international fair trial standards (ICCPR, ECHR, Inter-American Convention)
Assessment of judicial impartiality, independence, and absence of bias
Review of due process protections: notice, access to evidence, right to counsel, right to cross-examine witnesses
Evaluation of procedural transparency and public access to proceedings
Expert assessment by international human rights organizations and comparative law scholars
expectedMetrics: Compliance with International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR) Article 14 fair trial guarantees
Compliance with Inter-American Convention on Human Rights fair trial provisions
Judicial impartiality: absence of conflict of interest, structural bias, or predetermined outcome
Due process: adequate notice, access to evidence, effective defense representation, right to cross-examination
Transparency: public access to proceedings, reasoned judicial opinions, appellate review availability
Proportionality of precautionary measures and restrictions on defendant
expectedEvidenceProfile.primaryMetric: Compliance of STF proceedings with international fair trial standards under ICCPR Article 14 and Inter-American Convention
componentMetrics: Judicial impartiality and absence of conflict of interest
Right to adequate notice and access to evidence
Right to effective legal representation and counsel
Right to cross-examine witnesses and present defense
Reasoned judicial opinion and appellate review
Proportionality of precautionary measures
Transparency and public access to proceedings

atomicClaims.statement: The verdicts against Jair Bolsonaro met international standards for a fair trial.

methodologies: Analysis of verdict reasoning and evidentiary foundation against international standards
Assessment of proportionality of sentence and consistency with international sentencing norms
Review of appellate review availability and procedural safeguards post-verdict
Expert assessment of verdict's compliance with rule of law and due process principles
expectedMetrics: Evidentiary sufficiency: whether verdict is supported by evidence presented at trial (witness testimony, documentary evidence, electronic records)
Reasoned judgment: whether verdict contains detailed legal reasoning and factual findings
Proportionality: whether sentence is proportionate to offense and consistent with international standards
Appellate review: availability of appeal and higher court review mechanisms
Finality and res judicata: whether verdict follows proper conclusion of proceedings
expectedEvidenceProfile.primaryMetric: Compliance of STF verdict with international fair trial standards regarding evidentiary sufficiency, reasoned judgment, proportionality, and appellate review
componentMetrics: Evidentiary foundation: witness testimony, documentary evidence, electronic records (cell phone logs, building access logs, GPS tracking)
Judicial reasoning: detailed explanation of findings and legal conclusions
Proportionality of sentence to offense
Availability and accessibility of appellate review

distinctEvents.name: Federal Police indictment
distinctEvents.description: Federal Police indicted Jair Bolsonaro and 33 co-conspirators based on evidence including WhatsApp messages and other investigative materials related to alleged coup attempt.

distinctEvents.name: STF indictment acceptance
distinctEvents.description: Brazilian Supreme Court (STF) unanimously accepted the indictment against Bolsonaro, making him a defendant in the criminal proceedings.

distinctEvents.name: Trial proceedings and evidence phase
distinctEvents.description: Oral evidence production phase began with prosecution witnesses, followed by defense witnesses. At least 73 witnesses testified in September 2025, with cell phone conversations, building access logs, and GPS tracking data presented as evidence.

distinctEvents.name: STF verdict and conviction
distinctEvents.description: Brazilian Federal Supreme Court convicted Bolsonaro on charges of attempting a coup (Articles 359-L and 359-M of Brazilian Criminal Code), marking the first conviction of a former Brazilian president for this offense.

Finality and res judicata: whether verdict follows proper conclusion of proceedings
expectedEvidenceProfile.primaryMetric: Compliance of STF verdict with international fair trial standards regarding evidentiary sufficiency, reasoned judgment, proportionality, and appellate review
componentMetrics: Evidentiary foundation: witness testimony, documentary evidence, electronic records (cell phone logs, building access logs, GPS tracking)
Judicial reasoning: detailed explanation of findings and legal conclusions
Proportionality of sentence to offense
Availability and accessibility of appellate review

gate1Reasoning.reasoning: Factual assertion (compliance with law is verifiable), but lacks specificity: which proceedings (multiple cases exist), which aspects of Brazilian law, and what time period? The claim is too broad to target verification without narrowing scope.

Fails opinion: 'international standards for a fair trial' is inherently evaluative and contested—different frameworks (UN, ICC, regional courts) define fairness differently, and no single objective standard exists. Also fails specificity: which standards (ICCPR, ECHR, ICC standards?), which aspects of proceedings (evidence handling, judicial impartiality, right to counsel?), and measured against what baseline?

Fails opinion: same as AC_02—'international standards for a fair trial' is evaluative with no universally agreed definition. Also fails specificity: which verdicts (multiple convictions exist), which standards, and what measurable criteria? The claim conflates verdicts (outcomes) with procedural fairness, which are distinct dimensions.

contractValidationSummary.summary: The three claims correctly decompose the two priority anchors: AC_01 preserves 'comply with Brazilian law'; AC_02 and AC_03 split 'the proceedings and the verdicts' under 'international standards for a fair trial'. All truth-condition-bearing modifiers are preserved across thesis-direct claims. No normative injection detected. No proxy drift. No bundling or subsumption issues.

truthConditionAnchor.anchorText: comply with Brazilian law / meet international standards for a fair trial / the proceedings and the verdicts
preservedByQuotes: met international standards for a fair trial
The verdicts against Jair Bolsonaro met international standards for a fair trial

anchors.rationale: The specific standard of compliance (Brazilian law vs. other legal frameworks) determines what evidence is needed to verify the claim.
anchors.truthConditionShiftIfRemoved: Without specifying Brazilian law, the verification would need to assess compliance against an unspecified or different legal standard.

anchors.text: meet international standards for a fair trial
anchors.inputSpan: meet international standards for a fair trial
anchors.rationale: The specific standard invoked (international fair trial standards) is distinct from domestic compliance and requires different evidence.
anchors.truthConditionShiftIfRemoved: Without this standard, the claim would only concern domestic legality, not alignment with international norms.

anchors.rationale: The claim covers both the process and the outcomes; removing either would narrow what must be verified.
anchors.truthConditionShiftIfRemoved: Removing this distinction would allow verification to focus on only the trial process or only the final judgments, not both.

claimSelection.rationale: All three candidates are fact-check-worthy and directly address the two-part thesis (domestic legal compliance + international fair trial standards). AC_01 covers the Brazilian law compliance dimension exclusively. AC_02 and AC_03 both a...

evidenceItems.statement: The Supreme Federal Court found serious biases in the first case against Lula and nullified his two convictions in 2021, with the court also annulling all other pending cases.
evidenceItems.sourceTitle: Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva - Wikipedia
evidenceItems.sourceExcerpt: The Supreme Federal Court found serious biases in the first case against Lula and nullified his two convictions in 2021, with the court also annulling all other pending cases.

evidenceScope.boundaries: Operation Car Wash proceedings against Lula
evidenceScope.analyticalDimension: judicial bias and fairness of proceedings

evidenceItems.statement: On March 9, 2021, two judges of the Supreme Federal Court (Gilmar Mendes and Ricardo Lewandowski) voted that Sergio Moro was biased in cases against Lula.
evidenceItems.sourceExcerpt: On March 9, 2021, two judges of the Supreme Federal Court (Gilmar Mendes and Ricardo Lewandowski) voted that Sergio Moro was biased in cases against Lula.

evidenceItems.statement: In 2022, the United Nations Human Rights Committee agreed with the Brazilian Supreme Federal Court that Sergio Moro was biased in all cases against Lula.
evidenceItems.sourceExcerpt: In 2022, the United Nations Human Rights Committee agreed with the Brazilian Supreme Federal Court that Sergio Moro was biased in all cases against Lula.

evidenceScope.name: United Nations Human Rights Co
evidenceScope.methodology: United Nations Human Rights Committee review and determination
evidenceScope.boundaries: Operation Car Wash proceedings; judicial bias assessment
evidenceScope.analyticalDimension: international standards for judicial impartiality and fair trial

evidenceItems.statement: The United Nations Human Rights Committee published a conclusion on April 28, 2022, pointing out that Sergio Moro had been biased in the trial of cases against Lula, and determined that Brazil must present measures adopted to repair the damage caused within 180 days and prevent other people from being targeted by similar proceedings in the future.
evidenceItems.sourceTitle: Imprisonment of Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva - Wikipedia
evidenceItems.sourceExcerpt: The United Nations Human Rights Committee published a conclusion on April 28, 2022, pointing out that Sergio Moro had been biased in the trial of cases against Lula, and determined that Brazil must present measures adopted to repair the damage caused within 180 days and prevent other people from being targeted by similar proceedings in the future.

evidenceScope.analyticalDimension: international standards for judicial impartiality and fair trial

evidenceItems.statement: Justice Edson Fachin overturned Lula's convictions on March 8, 2021, ruling that the Federal Regional Court of the 4th Region had tried the case outside its jurisdiction, and Lula's convictions were annulled in 2021 due to jurisdictional bias.
evidenceItems.sourceTitle: Imprisonment of Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva - Wikipedia
evidenceItems.sourceExcerpt: Justice Edson Fachin overturned Lula's convictions on March 8, 2021, ruling that the Federal Regional Court of the 4th Region had tried the case outside its jurisdiction, and Lula's convictions were annulled in 2021 due to jurisdictional bias.

evidenceItems.statement: In August 2018, the United Nations Human Rights Committee requested that Brazil guarantee Lula the right to exercise his political rights while in prison, including access to members of his party and the media and participation in the 2018 presidential election.
evidenceItems.sourceTitle: Imprisonment of Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva - Wikipedia

evidenceItems.statement: The Igarapé Institute's October 2020 strategic paper documents that President Bolsonaro has personally dismissed ministers and secretaries that challenged his views or had harmed his self-interests, and notes a hypothesis that the Brazilian president may be using allies in office to prevent government officials from investigating his personal affairs, which would constitute abuse of power.
